content:
# Numbers Chapter 23 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 176-177 | Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0130-0131 (LXX)
**Source Images:** `NUM_023_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_023_page_002.jpg`; `NUM_023_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_023_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### Preparations for the First Oracle (vv. 1-6)

**1** And Balaam said to Balak, "Build for me here seven altars, and prepare for me here seven bulls and seven rams."

**2** And Balak did as Balaam had spoken. And Balak and Balaam offered up a bull and a ram on each altar.

**3** And Balaam said to Balak, "Station yourself by your burnt offering, and I will go. Perhaps YHWH will come to meet me, and whatever thing he shows me I will tell you." And he went to a bare height.

**4** And God met Balaam, and he said to him, "The seven altars I have arranged, and I have offered up a bull and a ram on each altar."

**5** And YHWH put a word in the mouth of Balaam and said, "Return to Balak, and thus you shall speak."

**6** And he returned to him, and behold, he was standing by his burnt offering, he and all the princes of Moab.

---

### Balaam's First Oracle (vv. 7-10)

**7** And he took up his discourse and said:

"From Aram, Balak has brought me,
the king of Moab from the mountains of the east:
'Come, curse Jacob for me,
and come, denounce Israel.'

**8** How shall I curse whom God has not cursed?
And how shall I denounce whom YHWH has not denounced?

**9** For from the top of the rocks I see him,
and from the hills I behold him.
Behold, a people dwelling alone,
and among the nations it does not reckon itself.

**10** Who can count the dust of Jacob,
or number the fourth part of Israel?
Let my soul die the death of the upright,
and let my end be like his!"

---

### Balak's Response and Preparations for the Second Oracle (vv. 11-17)

**11** And Balak said to Balaam, "What have you done to me? To curse my enemies I took you, and behold, you have surely blessed them!"

**12** And he answered and said, "Is it not that which YHWH puts in my mouth, that I must take heed to speak?"

**13** And Balak said to him, "Come, please, with me to another place from which you will see them. Only the end of them you shall see, but the whole of them you shall not see. And curse them for me from there."

**14** And he took him to the field of Zophim, to the top of Pisgah. And he built seven altars and offered up a bull and a ram on each altar.

**15** And he said to Balak, "Station yourself here by your burnt offering, and I will meet yonder."

**16** And YHWH met Balaam and put a word in his mouth and said, "Return to Balak, and thus you shall speak."

**17** And he came to him, and behold, he was standing by his burnt offering, and the princes of Moab with him. And Balak said to him, "What has YHWH spoken?"

---

### Balaam's Second Oracle (vv. 18-24)

**18** And he took up his discourse and said:

"Rise, Balak, and hear;
give ear to me, son of Zippor.

**19** God is not a man that he should lie,
nor a son of man that he should repent.
Has he said, and will he not do it?
And has he spoken, and will he not establish it?

**20** Behold, to bless I have received command;
and he has blessed, and I cannot reverse it.

**21** He has not beheld iniquity in Jacob,
and he has not seen trouble in Israel.
YHWH his God is with him,
and the shout of a king is among them.

**22** God, who brought them out from Egypt,
is for them like the horns of the wild ox.

**23** For there is no enchantment against Jacob,
and there is no divination against Israel.
At the appointed time it shall be said of Jacob
and of Israel, 'What has God wrought!'

**24** Behold, a people — as a lioness it rises up,
and as a lion it lifts itself.
It does not lie down until it devours the prey
and drinks the blood of the slain."

---

### Balak's Frustration and Preparations for the Third Oracle (vv. 25-30)

**25** And Balak said to Balaam, "Neither curse them at all, nor bless them at all!"

**26** And Balaam answered and said to Balak, "Did I not speak to you, saying, 'All that YHWH speaks, that I must do'?"

**27** And Balak said to Balaam, "Come, please, I will take you to another place. Perhaps it will be right in the eyes of God that you will curse them for me from there."

**28** And Balak took Balaam to the top of Peor, which overlooks the face of the Jeshimon.

**29** And Balaam said to Balak, "Build for me here seven altars, and prepare for me here seven bulls and seven rams."

**30** And Balak did as Balaam had said, and he offered up a bull and a ram on each altar.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Notes

1. **v. 1 -- "seven altars...seven bulls and seven rams" (שִׁבְעָה מִזְבְּחֹת...שִׁבְעָה פָרִים וְשִׁבְעָה אֵילִם):** The sevenfold pattern is characteristic of ancient Near Eastern ritual. The Leningrad Codex reads the standard Masoretic form. Seven is the number of completeness and oath (from the root שׁבע); the ritual preparation aims at securing a favorable divine encounter.

2. **v. 2 -- "Balak and Balaam offered up" (וַיַּעַל בָּלָק וּבִלְעָם):** The verb עָלָה in the Hiphil ("to offer up, to cause to ascend") indicates both the king and the diviner participated in the sacrifice. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders the verb with a form of ἀναφέρω, confirming the sacrificial sense.

3. **v. 3 -- "a bare height" (שֶׁפִי):** The Hebrew שֶׁפִי denotes a barren, wind-swept height. The Leningrad Codex has this reading clearly. The LXX renders εὐθεῖαν ("a straight/level place"), interpreting the terrain differently. Some traditions read this as a proper noun; here it is taken as a common noun describing the landscape Balaam ascended to seek a divine encounter.

4. **v. 7 -- "he took up his discourse" (וַיִּשָּׂא מְשָׁלוֹ):** The noun מָשָׁל here means "oracle, parable, discourse" -- a formal prophetic utterance. The verb נָשָׂא ("to take up, lift") is the standard formula for beginning an oracular pronouncement. This construction recurs at v. 18 for the second oracle and at 24:3, 15, 20, 21, 23 for subsequent oracles.

5. **v. 7 -- "From Aram" (מִן־אֲרָם):** Aram designates the Aramean region in upper Mesopotamia, identifying Balaam's homeland. This corresponds to "Pethor, which is by the River" in 22:5. The poetic parallelism pairs "Aram" with "mountains of the east" (הַרְרֵי־קֶדֶם), specifying the eastern highland region.

6. **v. 7 -- "curse...denounce" (קָבָה...זָעַם):** The first oracle uses two roots for cursing: קָבַב ("to curse, execrate") and זָעַם ("to denounce, express indignation"). These differ from the roots in 22:6 (אָרַר and קָבַב), expanding the semantic field of malediction. The formal equivalence renders these as "curse" and "denounce" to maintain the distinction.

7. **v. 8 -- "How shall I curse...how shall I denounce" (מָה אֶקֹּב...וּמָה אֶזְעֹם):** The rhetorical questions use the interrogative מָה in the sense of "how" (not "what"), expressing impossibility. Balaam cannot curse what God has not cursed. The LXX renders with τί ("what/how"), preserving the rhetorical structure.

8. **v. 9 -- "a people dwelling alone" (עָם לְבָדָד יִשְׁכֹּן):** This is one of the theologically weightiest lines in the Torah. The adverb לְבָדָד ("alone, in isolation") echoes Deuteronomy 33:28 (Moses' blessing). The people's separateness is not mere social isolation but covenantal distinction. The LXX renders λαὸς μόνος κατοικήσει ("a people alone shall dwell"), retaining the force.

9. **v. 10 -- "the dust of Jacob" (עֲפַר יַעֲקֹב):** An allusion to the patriarchal promise in Genesis 13:16 and 28:14, where Abraham's and Jacob's descendants are compared to the dust of the earth. The word עָפָר ("dust") is both a number metaphor (innumerable) and an echo of the covenant promises.

10. **v. 10 -- "the fourth part" (רֹבַע):** The Hebrew רֹבַע means "a fourth part, a quarter." The word is rare and appears only here and in a cognate form. Some interpreters relate it to רָבַב ("to be many"), but the Masoretic pointing supports "fourth part." The LXX renders ἀριθμόν ("number"), apparently reading or interpreting differently. The formal rendering follows the MT.

11. **v. 10 -- "Let my soul die the death of the upright" (תָּמֹת נַפְשִׁי מוֹת יְשָׁרִים):** The infinitive absolute construction (מוֹת...תָּמֹת, literally "let-die my soul the-death-of") is preserved in the rendering. The noun יְשָׁרִים ("upright ones") is a characterization of Israel. This wish by a pagan diviner to share in Israel's eschatological destiny is remarkable.

12. **v. 11 -- "you have surely blessed them" (בָּרֵךְ בֵּרָכְתָּ):** Infinitive absolute construction: בָּרֵךְ בֵּרָכְתָּ. Rendered with doubling ("surely blessed") per binding conventions.

13. **v. 13 -- "Only the end of them you shall see" (אֶפֶס קָצֵהוּ תִרְאֶה):** Balak's strategy is to limit Balaam's visual field, showing him only a portion (קָצֶה, "end, extremity") of Israel. The particle אֶפֶס functions as a restrictive adverb ("only, merely"). Balak apparently believes that seeing the full magnitude of Israel contributes to the blessing rather than the curse.

14. **v. 14 -- "the field of Zophim, to the top of Pisgah" (שְׂדֵה צֹפִים אֶל־רֹאשׁ הַפִּסְגָּה):** The "field of Zophim" means "field of the watchers/lookouts," an elevated vantage point. Pisgah is the ridge from which Moses will later view the Promised Land (Deuteronomy 34:1). The Leningrad Codex reading is clear. The LXX renders Pisgah as a transliteration (Φασγα).

15. **v. 19 -- "God is not a man that he should lie" (לֹא אִישׁ אֵל וִיכַזֵּב):** One of the most theologically significant statements in the Torah. The negation לֹא אִישׁ אֵל ("not a man is God") asserts divine transcendence over human unreliability. The verb כָּזַב ("to lie, be deceptive") and its parallel נָחַם ("to repent, change one's mind") together assert the immutability of divine intention. The LXX renders οὐχ ὡς ἄνθρωπος ὁ θεός ("not as a man is God").

16. **v. 19 -- "nor a son of man that he should repent" (וּבֶן־אָדָם וְיִתְנֶחָם):** The phrase בֶּן־אָדָם ("son of man/adam") here means simply "a human being." The verb נָחַם in the Hitpael means "to repent, relent, change one's mind." This verse is frequently cited in theological discussions of divine immutability (cf. 1 Samuel 15:29).

17. **v. 20 -- "to bless I have received command" (בָּרֵךְ לָקָחְתִּי):** Literally "to bless I have taken/received." The verb לָקַח here means "to receive (a commission)." The LXX renders εὐλογεῖν with a purpose infinitive. Balaam states that he has been divinely conscripted to bless, not curse.

18. **v. 21 -- "He has not beheld iniquity in Jacob" (לֹא־הִבִּיט אָוֶן בְּיַעֲקֹב):** The nouns אָוֶן ("iniquity, trouble") and עָמָל ("trouble, misery") are debated: does this mean God sees no sin in Israel (a declaration of innocence) or no calamity/disaster against Israel (a declaration of protection)? The Masoretic vocalization and the parallelism with "trouble" (עָמָל) favor the latter sense -- God has not gazed upon misfortune directed at Jacob. However, the formal equivalence preserves the ambiguity with "iniquity" and "trouble."

19. **v. 21 -- "the shout of a king" (וּתְרוּעַת מֶלֶךְ):** The noun תְּרוּעָה denotes a war cry or an acclamation shout. "A king" (מֶלֶךְ) is deliberately ambiguous: it may refer to YHWH as Israel's divine king or to a future human king. Given the context of YHWH's presence "with him" in the preceding line, the primary reference is to YHWH's royal acclamation among his people.

20. **v. 22 -- "like the horns of the wild ox" (כְּתוֹעֲפֹת רְאֵם):** The noun תּוֹעָפוֹת is debated. It likely means "horns" or "lofty strength." The רְאֵם is the now-extinct aurochs (Bos primigenius), a massive wild bovine symbolizing untamable power. The LXX renders ὡς δόξα μονοκέρωτος ("like the glory of a unicorn/one-horned beast"), reflecting the ancient Greek interpretation. The formal equivalence follows the MT sense of "wild ox."

21. **v. 23 -- "no enchantment...no divination" (לֹא־נַחַשׁ...וְלֹא־קֶסֶם):** The terms נַחַשׁ ("enchantment, omen-reading") and קֶסֶם ("divination") are the very practices Balaam was hired to perform. The oracle thus declares that Israel is immune to the arts of its own speaker -- a profound irony. The preposition בְּ (בְּיַעֲקֹב, בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל) means "against" in this context.

22. **v. 23 -- "What has God wrought!" (מַה־פָּעַל אֵל):** This exclamation is placed in the mouths of future observers. The verb פָּעַל ("to do, work, accomplish") emphasizes divine action. This phrase became famous in modern history as the first telegraph message (1844), though taken from the KJV rendering.

23. **v. 24 -- "as a lioness...as a lion" (כְּלָבִיא...וְכַאֲרִי):** Two different Hebrew words for lion are used in the poetic parallelism: לָבִיא ("lioness" or "great lion") and אֲרִי ("lion"). The imagery of a predator that will not rest until it has consumed its prey conveys Israel's irresistible military power as granted by God. The LXX renders with ὡς σκύμνος ("as a young lion") and ὡς λέων ("as a lion").

24. **v. 25 -- "Neither curse them at all, nor bless them at all" (גַּם קָבֹב לֹא תִקֳּבֶנּוּ גַּם בָּרֵךְ לֹא תְבָרְכֶנּוּ):** Double infinitive absolute constructions: קָבֹב...תִקֳּבֶנּוּ and בָּרֵךְ...תְבָרְכֶנּוּ. Balak's frustration is expressed through emphatic negation with infinitive absolutes. He would prefer silence to blessing. The rendering preserves the emphasis with "at all."

25. **v. 27 -- "Perhaps it will be right in the eyes of God" (אוּלַי יִישַׁר בְּעֵינֵי הָאֱלֹהִים):** The verb יָשַׁר ("to be straight, right, pleasing") with the prepositional phrase בְּעֵינֵי ("in the eyes of") is an idiom for divine approval. Balak's "perhaps" (אוּלַי) reveals his desperation -- he now acknowledges that the outcome depends on God's will, not Balaam's skill.

26. **v. 28 -- "the top of Peor, which overlooks the face of the Jeshimon" (רֹאשׁ הַפְּעוֹר הַנִּשְׁקָף עַל־פְּנֵי הַיְשִׁימֹן):** Peor is a mountain in the Abarim range. The Jeshimon (הַיְשִׁימֹן, "the wasteland, desolation") refers to the arid wilderness region. The participle נִשְׁקָף ("overlooking, gazing down upon") from שָׁקַף describes the commanding view from this height. This is the third vantage point, and it will also fail to produce a curse (ch. 24).

### Cross-References to LXX (Vaticanus)

- **v. 1:** The LXX agrees with the MT on the sevenfold altar-and-sacrifice preparation. Vaticanus reads ἑπτὰ βωμούς ("seven altars") and ἑπτὰ μόσχους καὶ ἑπτὰ κριούς ("seven calves and seven rams"), substituting μόσχους ("calves") for the MT's פָרִים ("bulls").
- **v. 3:** The LXX renders שֶׁפִי ("bare height") as εὐθεῖαν ("a straight/level place"), a divergent interpretation of the terrain.
- **v. 7:** The LXX preserves the poetic structure of the first oracle, rendering "from Mesopotamia" (ἐξ Μεσοποταμίας) for "from Aram," a geographic interpretation rather than a transliteration.
- **v. 9:** Both MT and LXX preserve the theologically critical phrase about Israel dwelling alone and not reckoning itself among the nations.
- **v. 10:** The LXX renders רֹבַע ("fourth part") differently, reading ἀριθμόν ("number"), suggesting a Vorlage or interpretive tradition that diverged from the MT at this point.
- **v. 13:** Both traditions agree on Balak's strategy of relocating to show Balaam only a portion of the Israelite camp.
- **v. 19:** The LXX rendering οὐχ ὡς ἄνθρωπος ὁ θεός ("not as a man is God") closely matches the MT's theological assertion, though the Greek syntax introduces a comparative particle (ὡς) not present in the Hebrew.
- **v. 21:** The LXX appears to interpret אָוֶן and עָמָל as "toil/suffering" rather than moral "iniquity," supporting the protective rather than forensic reading.
- **v. 22:** The LXX's μονοκέρως ("one-horned beast") for רְאֵם ("wild ox") reflects the standard Septuagintal rendering, which later influenced the "unicorn" tradition in European Bible translations.
- **v. 23:** Both MT and LXX agree on the inefficacy of enchantment and divination against Israel, though the LXX syntax varies slightly.
- **v. 24:** The LXX renders the lion imagery with σκύμνος and λέων, standard Greek equivalents, preserving the predatory metaphor.
- **v. 28:** The LXX transliterates "Peor" (Φογωρ) and renders "Jeshimon" as a common noun for "wilderness/desolation" (τὴν ἔρημον).

### Structural Observations

Numbers 23 is architecturally governed by a threefold ritual pattern: build seven altars, sacrifice, withdraw for divine encounter, return with oracle. The chapter completes two full cycles of this pattern (vv. 1-12, 13-26) and initiates the third (vv. 27-30), which will culminate in the oracle of chapter 24.

The two oracles form a progressive intensification. The first oracle (vv. 7-10) declares what God has NOT done (not cursed, not denounced) and describes Israel's character (dwelling alone, innumerable). The second oracle (vv. 18-24) advances to what God IS and DOES: God is not a man (v. 19), God brought them from Egypt (v. 22), God makes them invincible against sorcery (v. 23), and God empowers them like a lion (v. 24). The movement is from negative protection to positive empowerment.

The mouth/word motif from chapter 22 continues as the central theological thread. YHWH "puts a word" in Balaam's mouth (vv. 5, 16); Balaam must speak what YHWH puts in his mouth (v. 12); and the oracles themselves are YHWH's words issuing from a pagan diviner's mouth. The irony deepens: Balak hired a mouth to curse, but that mouth has been commandeered by YHWH to bless.

Balak's three relocations (Bamoth-baal in 22:41, Pisgah in 23:14, Peor in 23:28) trace a geographic arc across the Moabite highlands, each offering a different vantage point over the Israelite camp. The strategy assumes that visual perspective affects divinatory power -- that seeing less of Israel might weaken the blessing. This assumption is systematically defeated: each new location produces a more powerful blessing than the last.
